questions.question: What is the core difference between a window function and GROUP BY aggregation, and when do you use each?
options: Window functions work on text data; GROUP BY works on numbers — they handle different data types.

GROUP BY collapses multiple rows into one row per group (you lose individual row data). Window functions perform the same calculation but KEEP all original rows — each row also shows the computation over its partition. Use GROUP BY when you want: totals per department, employee count per location. Use window functions when you want: each employee's salary AND their department average AND their rank in the department — all in one result set without joining aggregated subqueries.
Window functions require an ORDER BY clause; GROUP BY never uses ORDER BY.
GROUP BY is standard SQL; window functions are PostgreSQL-specific extensions.
questions.answer: GROUP BY collapses multiple rows into one row per group (you lose individual row data). Window functions perform the same calculation but KEEP all original rows — each row also shows the computation over its partition. Use GROUP BY when you want: totals per department, employee count per location. Use window functions when you want: each employee's salary AND their department average AND their rank in the department — all in one result set without joining aggregated subqueries.
questions.explanation: Before window functions, getting 'each employee plus their department average' required: SELECT e.*, da.avg_salary FROM employees e JOIN (SELECT department, AVG(salary) as avg_salary FROM employees GROUP BY department) da ON e.department = da.department. Window functions make this: SELECT *, AVG(salary) OVER (PARTITION BY department) FROM employees. Same result, no subquery, one scan.

questions.question: In a recursive CTE for organizational hierarchy, what prevents an infinite loop if there's a cycle in the data (employee A manages B, B manages A)?
options: PostgreSQL automatically detects cycles and stops recursive CTEs.

By default, PostgreSQL will infinite-loop and crash / run until max_recursion depth. Prevention options: (1) Add a depth limit: WHERE h.level < 10, (2) Track visited IDs in an array: array_append(h.visited, e.id) and check NOT (e.id = ANY(h.visited)), (3) Use PostgreSQL's CYCLE clause (added in PostgreSQL 14): WITH RECURSIVE ... CYCLE id SET is_cycle USING path — automatically detects cycles and marks them. Always design hierarchical data to prevent cycles at the constraint level.
Recursive CTEs in PostgreSQL are limited to 3 levels of recursion by default.
The UNION ALL keyword prevents cycles — use UNION DISTINCT to allow cycles.
questions.answer: By default, PostgreSQL will infinite-loop and crash / run until max_recursion depth. Prevention options: (1) Add a depth limit: WHERE h.level < 10, (2) Track visited IDs in an array: array_append(h.visited, e.id) and check NOT (e.id = ANY(h.visited)), (3) Use PostgreSQL's CYCLE clause (added in PostgreSQL 14): WITH RECURSIVE ... CYCLE id SET is_cycle USING path — automatically detects cycles and marks them. Always design hierarchical data to prevent cycles at the constraint level.
questions.explanation: PostgreSQL 14+ CYCLE syntax: WITH RECURSIVE org(id, name, manager_id, path) AS ( ... UNION ALL ... ) CYCLE id SET is_cycle USING path SELECT * FROM org WHERE NOT is_cycle. The database automatically tracks the path and stops when it detects a repeated ID. For pre-14 PostgreSQL: the visited array approach is the safest.

questions.question: Why does PostgreSQL's JSONB outperform JSON column type, and when should you use JSONB vs a normalized relational schema?
options: JSONB supports more JSON data types than JSON — JSON can't store numbers.

JSON stores data as raw text (fast writes, slow reads — must re-parse every query). JSONB stores binary-encoded, pre-parsed JSON (slower writes, fast reads — no parsing overhead, supports GIN indexes for deep attribute queries). JSONB also deduplicates keys and removes insignificant whitespace. Use JSONB when: attributes vary widely per entity (EAV pattern alternative), schema is not yet known, data from external APIs. Use normalized relational tables when: frequently querying/joining on specific attributes, data integrity constraints needed (foreign keys, NOT NULL), or complex reporting queries are required.
JSONB is standard SQL; JSON is a PostgreSQL-specific extension.
JSONB stores data in a separate table for performance; JSON stores inline.
questions.answer: JSON stores data as raw text (fast writes, slow reads — must re-parse every query). JSONB stores binary-encoded, pre-parsed JSON (slower writes, fast reads — no parsing overhead, supports GIN indexes for deep attribute queries). JSONB also deduplicates keys and removes insignificant whitespace. Use JSONB when: attributes vary widely per entity (EAV pattern alternative), schema is not yet known, data from external APIs. Use normalized relational tables when: frequently querying/joining on specific attributes, data integrity constraints needed (foreign keys, NOT NULL), or complex reporting queries are required.
questions.explanation: Common use: e-commerce products where different product types have different attributes. A shoe has {size, color, material}, a laptop has {RAM, storage, screen_size}. Storing these in a normalized EAV (Entity-Attribute-Value) table requires complex multi-join queries. JSONB lets each product row store its own schema while still being queryable: WHERE attributes @> '{"color": "red"}' with a GIN index is nearly as fast as a dedicated indexed column.
